text: In Letter I, Seneca introduces a taxonomy of time-thieves that still holds. Some hours, he says, are seized by others — the morning caller, the demanding patron. Some are stolen by circumstance — illness, obligations we never chose. But most, he insists, simply slip away: 'fluunt et labuntur' — they flow and glide. This third category is most dangerous because it leaves no visible wound. Seneca's counsel is radical accounting: collect the hours the way a miser counts coins, because time, unlike money, cannot be borrowed or repaid once gone.
question: According to Seneca's taxonomy, which category of time loss is most dangerous?

options.text: Hours seized by demanding social obligations
Hours lost to illness and unchosen circumstance
Hours that simply flow and glide away unnoticed
Hours wasted in hollow philosophical debate
